regressions.reasoning: Stage 06 rotates the RabbitMQ TLS certificate, which requires rolling restarts of all RabbitMQ pods; this pod cycling would temporarily take app-producer out of Ready state and could leave it in a non-Ready state if the rolling restart disrupted connectivity. The app-producer's readiness depends on RabbitMQ being reachable, and certificate rotation with node restarts is a legitimate later-stage action that disrupts the stage_01 prerequisite without the agent being careless.

regressions.reasoning: Stage 06 performs a TLS leaf-certificate rotation that requires rolling restarts of all three RabbitMQ pods; if those restarts left any pod or the app-client/ops-client deployments in an unhealthy state, the stage_02 oracle would timeout waiting for Ready conditions rather than return a clean pass. No later stage was legitimately designed to remove user permissions or disrupt client connectivity — every subsequent stage either adds read-only ConfigMaps (stages 04, 07, 09) or re-applies mirroring policy (stages 03, 08) — so the timeout is a real side-effect of the agent's work in stage_06 breaking the cluster health that stage_02 established.

Stage_06 performs a TLS certificate rotation that requires rolling restarts of all RabbitMQ pods and likely also restarts the Prometheus deployment or disrupts the oracle-client container — the 'deleted state' and 'container not found' errors indicate pods were cycled after stage_05 passed. The monitoring infrastructure (oracle-client pod, Prometheus exec targets) being gone is a side effect of the pod restarts from TLS rotation in stage_06, not careless breakage by the agent in stage_05.
